title: Dagging and crutching sheep
subtitle: Removing soiled wool from the hindquarters before fly season.
excerpt: Dag-matted wool around the breech is the main attractant for blowflies and the primary cause of fly-strike in a small flock. Removing it in late spring, before flies are active, is one of the most effective preventive steps a smallholder can take. This covers catching, the cutting pattern, and what to do with the clips.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code for Sheep (2023). AHDB Beef and Lamb, blowfly control guidance. John Seymour, New Complete Book of Self-Sufficiency (Dorling Kindersley, 1976), sheep management.

content.text: Blowfly populations peak in the UK from May to September.
on a soiled breech can be established within 24 hours of egg-laying on a warm day.
in April, before flies are active, removes the primary attractant and is faster than any chemical prevention that follows.

-check as a good reason to handle the whole flock, not just those with obvious soiling. Work them through a pen to a corner or race, then catch individually with a shepherd's crook (neck crook to stop a moving sheep, leg crook if you can get close enough from a still position). Once caught, guide the animal to a corner and hold it between your legs, head pointing away.

For dagging, it is easier to sit the sheep on its rump (the standard shearing position) than to try to work on a standing animal. Stand at the sheep's right side, reach across the back, hold the far hind leg, and tilt the animal back onto its rump. A sheep sat on its rump with its back resting against your legs cannot kick and is much easier to work on. Most sheep go quiet in this position.

Use hand shears (spring-action blade shears). Clear the wool from the full breech area: between the back legs, around the tail base, and across the inner thighs. The goal is a clean bare patch from the vulva or prepuce to the base of the tail. Extend the clipping 10 to 15 cm onto each inner thigh if the soiling is heavy. Cut close but do not nick the skin: the shears are sharp and skin cuts attract flies.
While the sheep is sat, check the hooves for overgrowth and trim if needed using the

. Apply a long-acting blowfly prevention product (Crovect, Dysect, or Clik, all prescription products from the vet) to the clipped area if blowfly season is underway. The combination of dagging and chemical prevention gives better protection than either alone.
